meta.title: Mutation vs immutable copy for shared JavaScript state
meta.summary: A UI updates nested client-side state and also needs undo, predictable re-renders, and easy debugging. Would you mutate existing objects or create immutable copies?

scenario: You are building a browser-based task board in JavaScript. When a user moves a card, the UI updates the card list, shows a recent activity feed, and keeps a simple undo history so the last action can be reversed. One teammate wants to mutate the existing state objects in place because it feels simpler and avoids extra copying. Another teammate wants to create new objects and arrays for each update so state transitions stay easier to trace and compare. In the interview, the panel asks which direction you would defend.
prompt: Which direction would you defend for this prompt, and how would you explain when the other option is still the better answer?

options.label: Mutate existing state
options.summary: Fits best when the data is short-lived, local, and not being compared, replayed, or shared across state boundaries.
whenItWins: The value is a temporary local object inside one function and no other part of the app depends on previous snapshots.
You are doing straightforward procedural work where mutating a local accumulator keeps the code small and clear.
There is no need for undo, history, change detection by reference, or reasoning about previous versus next state.
watchOutFor: Once the same object is shared across UI state, history, or multiple consumers, in-place mutation can make changes harder to notice and harder to debug.
A weak answer chooses mutation only because copying sounds wasteful, without checking whether the state needs predictable transitions or snapshot boundaries.

options.label: Create immutable copies
options.summary: Fits best when the app benefits from clear before-and-after state snapshots instead of in-place changes.
whenItWins: The state is shared across UI surfaces and you want updates to produce a clear next snapshot rather than silently changing the old one.
The product needs undo, time-travel-like debugging, change detection, or simpler reasoning about what changed.
You want update code that makes the transition from previous state to next state explicit.
watchOutFor: Blindly copying everything everywhere can add noise if the value is only a tiny local temporary variable that never crosses a state boundary.
A weak answer says immutable updates are always better without explaining what snapshot or predictability problem they are solving.

decisionMatrix.title: Local temporary working data
decisionMatrix.prompt: The value only lives inside a small function and no one needs the previous version after the work is done.

cells.note: This is easier to defend because the data is local, short-lived, and not acting like shared app state.
It still works, but now you should explain why extra snapshot ceremony helps a value that never really leaves the function.

decisionMatrix.title: The exact prompt in this battle
decisionMatrix.prompt: The app updates shared UI state and also needs undo history and clearer before-and-after state transitions.

cells.note: Possible, but you now take on more risk around debugging, history, and accidentally changing data that other parts of the app still reference.
This is easiest to defend because the prompt benefits directly from explicit snapshots and predictable state transitions.

decisionMatrix.title: Tight local transformation loop
decisionMatrix.prompt: You are doing local data processing in a hot path before producing one final result.

cells.note: This can be the cleaner answer when the mutations stay local and you only expose the final value once.
Still viable, but you should justify the extra copying if the work is purely local and snapshot history is not part of the problem.

evaluationDimensions.title: Is the value local data or shared state?
evaluationDimensions.description: This is the first question. Local temporary data tolerates mutation much better than shared app state that multiple features read from.

evaluationDimensions.title: Do you need previous versus next snapshots?
evaluationDimensions.description: Undo, debugging, optimistic rollback, and predictable updates all get easier when old and new state are clearly separate values.

evaluationDimensions.title: How will the rest of the system notice updates?
evaluationDimensions.description: Many UI patterns become easier to reason about when updates produce new references instead of mutating old ones in place.

evaluationDimensions.title: How easy is it to explain what changed?
evaluationDimensions.description: A strong answer should care about whether the next developer can understand a state transition without hunting for hidden mutations.

evaluationDimensions.title: How much structure is worth it here?
evaluationDimensions.description: Immutable updates add ceremony, so the question is whether that cost buys real clarity and safety for this exact prompt.

strongAnswer.title: A strong answer here would lean immutable copies because the prompt describes shared UI state with history and rollback needs
strongAnswer.summary: This task board is not just doing local data processing. It is updating shared UI state, keeping an undo history, and benefiting from clear previous-versus-next snapshots. That makes immutable copies easier to defend.
reasoning: If this were just a local helper function building one result and then returning it, I would be fine mutating a local temporary object because the mutation would stay contained.
In this prompt, the state lives longer and serves more jobs: rendering the board, updating activity, and supporting undo. That makes explicit next-state snapshots much more valuable.

Creating immutable copies makes it easier to see what changed, preserve older state for rollback, and avoid surprising side effects when multiple parts of the app still hold references to the old objects.
strongAnswer.recommendation: For this exact prompt, I would update the board with immutable copies and keep mutation for truly local temporary work where no snapshot, undo, or shared-state boundary exists.

interviewerPushback.question: Is mutation always bad in JavaScript?
interviewerPushback.answer: No. Mutation is often completely fine for local temporary values inside one function. The risk rises when the same object becomes shared state that multiple parts of the app depend on over time.

interviewerPushback.question: When would mutation clearly be the better answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: When the data is short-lived, local, and not being compared, replayed, or stored for history. In that case mutation can be simpler and perfectly reasonable.

interviewerPushback.question: What would change your answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: If the product no longer needed undo, old snapshots, or predictable shared-state transitions, and the work became a local transformation step, I would be more comfortable using mutation.

answerExamples.title: Weak answer
answerExamples.answer: I would never mutate because mutation is bad.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: It does not. This sounds absolute and teaches no boundary for when local mutation is still a perfectly good tool.

answerExamples.title: Decent answer
answerExamples.answer: I would probably create immutable copies because they are safer for state updates.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This is directionally fine, but it still needs a clearer explanation of what safety means in this prompt and when mutation remains reasonable.

answerExamples.title: Strong answer
answerExamples.answer: For this prompt I would create immutable copies because the board state is shared across the UI and the product also needs undo history. I want each update to produce a clear next snapshot instead of changing the previous objects in place. That makes it easier to debug what changed and easier to roll back if needed. I would still mutate local temporary values inside a helper when no one needs old snapshots and the data never becomes shared app state.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This sounds strong because it avoids dogma, explains why the prompt benefits from snapshots, and still leaves a healthy place for local mutation.

answerFramework: Start by asking whether the value is local temporary data or shared app state.
Check whether the problem needs old-versus-new snapshots for undo, rollback, debugging, or change detection.
Choose the style that matches that boundary.
Explain the cost of the alternative, either hidden shared mutations or unnecessary copy ceremony.
End with a simple rule of thumb, for example: mutate local temporaries, prefer immutable copies for shared state transitions.
antiPatterns: Saying mutation is always bad without explaining scope.
Saying copying is always wasteful without considering shared state, history, or rollback.
Ignoring whether the rest of the UI still holds references to the old objects.
Talking only about style preference instead of talking about state boundaries and snapshot needs.
